segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a glitchy, abstract transition featuring fragmented visuals and the WSJ logo, as a first female voiceover begins, "Behind the technology that consumers know and experience." This is immediately followed by a close-up of a hand tapping a payment terminal displaying a $1.00 transaction, accompanied by a distinct 'tap' sound. The scene transitions to a laptop screen showing a detailed stadium seating map, overlaid with a live concert crowd, while the voiceover asks, "Lies one simple question. How does it work?" A hand then holds a smartphone displaying Google Maps with a route from San Francisco to Union Square, set against a city street, illustrating the voiceover's point about "sophisticated software that gets us from point A to point B." This is followed by a dynamic, animated starfield with song titles like 'Anti-Hero' and 'Karma' labeled, visually representing "algorithms that suggest a specific song along the way." The narrative continues with visuals of Bose headphones labeled 'Outer Microphone,' a close-up of a human eye with colorful dots, and a man in a dark shirt working at a table with tools, as the voiceover explains, "We explore the engineering and science of technology that catches our eye, and speak to key players that make it all happen." A 'whoosh' sound accompanies a transition to a split-screen showing a person holding a phone with a route map on the left and a vibrant abstract background on the right. A woman in a red top is then seen using Google Maps on her phone, aligning with the voiceover's new focus: "Let's start here, with how Google remapped the world." A new, second female voiceover begins, explaining, "If you need to get somewhere, let's say a new building. Put it into maps and Google gives you a route in seconds," as a close-up of a hand typing 'Beverly Hills' into a search bar visually demonstrates this. A dynamic collage of transportation images—traffic lights, trains, and highways—appears, illustrating the complexity described by the second voiceover: "But behind that seemingly simple answer is a complex system of data, collected from users, satellites, cars, even camels." The video shows a woman walking while using Google Maps on her phone, followed by a computer screen displaying Google Maps directions from '123 main street, chicago' to 'chicago airport,' with options for 'By car' and 'By public transit.' The segment concludes with a woman in a purple jacket using augmented reality navigation on her phone, with a blue arrow guiding her path on the screen, all while the second voiceover highlights that "Today, more than 1 billion people use Google Maps every month. And in 17 years, it's evolved from a desktop application to a massive mobile platform." Throughout the minute, a soft, electronic background music plays, becoming slightly more prominent at the transition to the Google Maps focus.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a gray polo shirt speaking directly to the camera in a lab-like setting, surrounded by robotic devices, including a blue and black robot labeled 'Google Maps'. He explains that Google Maps has "over 150 million people around the world that are contributing information," with "50 million updates a day," urging viewers to "think of the map as being alive." A female narrator then interjects, noting that the extensive data collection has also attracted "criticism from privacy experts." The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying augmented reality navigation, showing a real-world street view with a blue arrow overlay, followed by an animated woman using her phone for navigation and a hand interacting with a smartphone map. In a workshop, a man in a blue shirt adjusts a robotic camera device, leading to a shot of a mounted camera system in a red-walled room. A title card, 'THE TECH BEHIND GOOGLE MAPS,' appears over a colorful abstract background with a whoosh sound, as the narrator states, "This is the tech behind Google Maps." Light background music begins. The video then shows a retro-style Google Maps interface from the early 2000s, with a hand typing search queries. A male voice describes its functionality, saying, "You can search for a place, like an address, an intersection, almost anything." The interface switches between 'Map,' 'Satellite,' and 'Hybrid' views, and a hand clicks on 'Street View.' The narrator adds that while Google wasn't the first in digital mapping, its mid-2000s launch brought "technology many people had never seen before." The video cuts to a man in a vest and glasses speaking in an office with world maps, who reminisces, "I remember the day that Google Maps launched with satellite imagery in the browser. Everybody just went and looked at their own house." The segment concludes with him working at a desk and a shot of globes on a shelf, emphasizing the global scope of mapping technology.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand using a black computer mouse on a vintage-style map mousepad. A female narrator introduces Craig Dalton, an Associate Professor of Geography at Hofstra University, who studies data-driven geotechnology. Dalton, a bald man with glasses, speaks animatedly in his office adorned with large world maps, including a political map of Africa, explaining that "No one had ever seen their own house in satellite imagery, much less in color satellite imagery before. This was a new view on the world." The scene transitions to a hand placing a red pushpin on a physical map of California, followed by a screenshot of a Google blog post from May 28, 2009, detailing the acquisition of Where 2 Tech, and a Wall Street Journal headline from October 27, 2004, announcing Google’s acquisition of Keyhole. The narrator highlights these early acquisitions as key to Maps' success. Dalton elaborates, stating that "By buying Keyhole, they got the software that it took to have a easy, ready-to-use satellite imagery viewer on a global scale. Where 2 Technologies provided them a much better user interface than it was available elsewhere," adding that Google's capital allowed them to develop these further. As upbeat, light background music begins, the video showcases the progression of Google Maps: a 2007 web version with Street View, a smartphone displaying navigation to 'Frankies Spuntino,' and a car’s dashboard screen showing route options to Shenandoah National Park. The narrator notes Maps' quick tech expansion, including developer integration, Street View, and early Android and iOS apps. The final scenes transition to a workshop environment with red walls and exposed beams, where Christopher Phillips, identified as the current head of Google's Geo division, gestures toward robotic camera devices, including one labeled 'Google Maps,' under a 'Fleet Assembly' banner, as the narrator asks how all this came together to get users from point A to point B.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Christopher Phillips, General Manager, GEO at Google, speaking in a workshop environment with red walls and various robotic camera devices, including a blue and black 'Google Maps' robot, visible behind him. Phillips explains that the "foundational piece was to have a real-world model," a "digital truth of what's going on in the real world, including where streets are." A female narrator then suggests taking a look at a "real route in San Francisco that Chris walked us through." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of a grand, illuminated building, likely Union Station in Los Angeles, under a dusky sky, before cutting to hands holding a Smartphone displaying a Google Maps route to 'Frankies Spuntino.' Phillips describes a hypothetical scenario: "Let's say I'm in the San Francisco Ferry Building, the Farmers Market, and I need to get to San Francisco International Airport." The narrator elaborates that to create this "real-world model," Google "starts with satellite and aerial imagery" and layers it "together with data from Street View." A split-screen comparison then appears, showing Satellite and Aerial Imagery of a coastal city alongside a Street View of a city intersection, with the word 'PHOTOGRAMMETRY' overlaid as the narrator explains, "To do this, they use a process called photogrammetry," which, combined with GPS data, helps "pinpoint the right coordinates for the image." The narrator then invites a "quick detour off our route to France" to see the layering process using the "Arc de Triomphe," as a detailed 3D aerial view of a city square with a central monument, resembling the Arc de Triomphe, is shown. Phillips likens this process to "a big jigsaw puzzle where we can put these images together," understanding "the distance between images" and their "actual location, the GPS location." The video returns to Phillips speaking with expressive gestures, and concludes with a shot of a robotic camera device labeled 'Street View on Google Maps' and the colorful Google 'G' logo, while the narrator notes that Google keeps a "range of the cameras that have been used over time to capture imagery at the Street View garage on its campus." The WSJ logo remains visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a workshop with red walls and exposed beams, under a 'Fleet Assembly' banner, where Christopher Phillips, General Manager, GEO at Google, in a gray polo shirt, gestures as he speaks about the origins of Google's mapping technology, noting it "all started with a 500-pound camera that was forklift onto a van." Robotic camera devices, including a blue and black one labeled 'Google Maps' and another with a green top, are displayed. A female narrator then explains how viewers "may have seen a Street View car at some point," transitioning to outdoor shots of Google Street View cars—a silver Hyundai and a blue car with 'Google Street View' branding—driving through suburban and urban streets. Close-ups reveal their intricate camera systems, including a blue dome with multiple lenses and sensors, which the narrator details are equipped with a "360-degree camera on top," "LIDAR sensors" to measure distance, and "GPS to get an accurate location." To reach "more remote places," Google has "adapted cameras to fit the terrain," putting them "on backpacks" and using "transportation like bikes and snowmobiles," as a Google Street View bike and a red and black Segway-like vehicle with camera rigs are shown in the workshop. The narrator also mentions "rarer cases" involving "camels, divers, and even astronauts." A brief digital overlay shows a Google Street View interface for the United Arab Emirates. Back in the workshop, a man in a blue shirt adjusts a camera rig, while Christopher Phillips returns to describe their "most recent" innovation: a "15-pound, very lightweight" camera that "can be powered off of a phone versus a large computing unit that's in the car." The narrator concludes by stating this imagery "isn't just for looking at" but "helps keep their maps up to date by detecting..." as the segment ends with a satellite view of a coastal city overlaid with blue lines indicating mapped streets, all accompanied by a light, informative background music. The WSJ logo remains in the top right, and 'GOOGLE' appears in the top left during outdoor scenes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Craig Dalton, an Associate Professor at Hofstra University, speaking in his office adorned with world maps. He explains how Google Maps collects highly granular data, linking it to individual street addresses, people, and personal accounts like Gmail or Wi-Fi networks. The scene transitions to a 3D animated cityscape built atop a Google Maps interface, followed by a Wall Street Journal headline from October 23, 2010, 'Google’s Street View Cars Grabbed Private Data,' displayed on a smartphone showing Google Maps. A female narrator details how Google has faced criticism and lawsuits for collecting excessive data, capturing private property in Street View, and acquiring data from unsecured Wi-Fi networks via its cars, though Google claims the traffic and busyness data it uses is anonymized. The video then cuts to Christopher Phillips, General Manager, GEO at Google, in a workshop environment surrounded by robotic camera devices, including one labeled 'Google Maps.' He explains Google's use of 'differential privacy' technology to separate individual user activity from aggregated data collected from millions, which is then used to derive insights on busyness and travel times. The scene returns to a woman with red nail polish using Google Maps on her smartphone in a car, navigating to Shenandoah National Park, and later searching for museums. The female narrator clarifies that while some data used for personalized maps (like past locations) isn't anonymized, Google states it's not sold or shared with third parties and that users are given privacy controls.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated, top-down view of a basketball arena, highlighting numbered sections as a glowing white circle moves across the court. A female narrator explains that wristbands are pre-programmed to be part of different groups for specific effects, such as 'group one for one effect' or 'group two for another.' The scene transitions to a live, dimly lit stadium filled with a massive crowd, where Averil Bott, identified by the narrator as an 'operator' and 'conductor,' is seated at a control station high above the arena. She operates a complex lighting console with illuminated buttons and a laptop displaying a detailed stadium layout, managing synchronized LED wristbands worn by the audience. The narrator details how programmed effects activate different groups of wristbands to light up in sync, creating patterns within the stadium, or how Averil can manually control them, flashing colors like 'Knicks colors. Orange, blue, orange, blue.' Amidst crowd cheers and music, Averil Bott speaks, expressing her enjoyment of seeing 'the crowd get into it and be excited,' noting that 'you're having them be part of the show, which is which is special.' The narrator then mentions the signal emits from a 'really small box' before the scene cuts to Vincent Leclerc, Co-Founder and CEO of PIXMOB, speaking in a modern office. He describes their 'very simple transmitter,' which is 'like the size of a tissue box' and can connect to a light board, emphasizing that 'you can literally travel with the whole control system in your suitcase.' The video returns to the stadium, now showcasing a spectacular synchronized light display across the crowd, with the text 'XYLOBANDS' appearing on screen. The narrator concludes by explaining that RF technology is what 'most other wearable LED companies use too, like Xylobands,' as the lights form intricate patterns and waves, transforming the arena into a dazzling spectacle of color and motion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark stage illuminated by glowing blue circular lights, featuring 'XYLOBANDS' and 'WSJ' branding, as a female narrator explains that the technology is used for events from Eurovision to corporate gatherings. The scene transitions to a massive stadium filled with a crowd waving LED wristbands, creating a synchronized sea of blue, red, and white lights, with the 'PIXMOB' logo appearing on screen. The narrator highlights these as 'advanced wristbands' using infrared technology, seen at events like the Super Bowl or Lady Gaga. Vincent Leclerc, Co-Founder and CEO of PIXMOB, then appears in a modern office, explaining that while infrared technology is '50 years old' and 'really old school tech,' they've 'twisted it' to be 'quite new and magical.' He elaborates that infrared allows them to send specific data to individual locations, like 'to you or to the person next to you,' enabling 'specialized effects' that can turn a crowd into a 'video canvas.' The visuals return to aerial and wide-angle views of the 'RAYMOND JAMES STADIUM' crowd creating dynamic light patterns, while a technician's hand adjusts a black PIXMOB control device. The narrator resumes, stating that 'these signals come from robotic transmitters placed all over an arena,' as close-ups show wristbands glowing in various colors, including one with a '21 DAY FREQUENCY' tag. A technician is then seen at a control station with a laptop displaying a circular stadium diagram, managing the light show, followed by a 3D model of the stadium with animated light patterns. The narrator references a Coldplay concert where transmitters were placed on sound towers and the stage, concluding by mentioning 'Pixmob's visualizer' for designing these shows.

The final frames return to the abstract visualization of green dots forming a network against a dark background, emphasizing connectivity and data flow.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a Wall Street Journal title card from March 6, 2014, announcing Spotify's acquisition of Echo Nest, as a female narrator explains how Echo Nest blended machine learning and natural language processing to build a database of songs and artists, marking an important step in Spotify's recommendation system. A close-up shows a hand interacting with a smartphone displaying Spotify's dark-themed interface, navigating through playlists like 'Discover Weekly' and 'On Repeat'. With light, inquisitive background music beginning, the narrator asks how the system works, introducing "collaborative filtering." Ziad Sultan, Vice President of Personalization at Spotify, appears in a modern office, wearing a mustard-yellow sweater and glasses, explaining in a clear voice that "collaborative filtering looks at the pattern across all of this data and tries to understand when do tracks happen to be playlisted together very often," likening it to "building a map of music and podcasts." He is shown with a digital visualization of green dots behind him, representing a data model. The narrator then describes this "map" as each point representing a different track in Spotify's catalog, determined by collaborative filtering. Ziad Sultan further elaborates that this means tracks go together "according to the way users have playlisted them and listened to them." A split-screen then showcases two smartphones displaying curated niche playlists titled 'ASIAN TUMBLR 2006-2010'S' and 'asian american youtube musicians from the late 2000s / early 2010s'. An animated data visualization of a network of green dots, representing songs and artists, dynamically zooms and pans across the network, with labels such as 'Anti-Hero' and 'Karma' by Taylor Swift, and 'Black and Yellow' by Wiz Khalifa, illustrating how frequently playlisted songs are closer together. A digital world map with green data points labeled 'IN', 'UK', 'US', and 'NON-DIASPORIC' is briefly shown within this visualization. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant, abstract digital background displaying 'THE TECH BEHIND', which then transitions to 'TAP-TO-PAY' as a female narrator explains this is "the tech behind tap to pay" after mentioning its slow traction in the US. A hand then inserts a card into a black payment terminal, triggering a distinct "beep" sound, as its blue screen changes from 'INSERT TAP OR SLIDE CARD' to 'APPROVED.' The narrator introduces "NFC, or Near Field Communication," as the core technology, further clarifying it's a form of "RFID, or radio frequency identification," while a close-up of the terminal with 'NEAR FIELD COMMUNICATION' overlaid is shown, followed by a diagram linking 'NFC' to 'RFID' and 'RADIO FREQUENCY IDENTIFICATION.' A hand uses a white card to unlock a door with a digital lock, illustrating the narrator's point that viewers might be familiar with this technology for "unlocking your hotel room." A bright yellow card with '@Mike' and a smiley face is tapped against a smartphone, while the narrator explains that "NFC relies on those little wires, which are actually antennas." Mike MacLennan, General Manager of Hardware at Square, appears in a modern office with exposed brick and large windows, gesturing as he explains in a clear, explanatory tone that "the antenna kind of looks like a racetrack and it's sort of a coil of different wires, and that's what's used to basically transmit the radio frequency on both sides." The female narrator formally introduces "Mike MacLennan," identifying him as "the General Manager of Hardware at Square, a company that makes card readers." Mike is then shown disassembling a white payment terminal on a table, revealing its intricate internal components like a circuit board and screen, as the narrator states he "cracked one open to show us exactly how a tap to pay transaction works." Mike humorously adds, "Reminds me a lot of opening oysters." As Mike continues to handle the disassembled device, the narrator reiterates that "Card readers also have those antennas," and Mike points out, "This is the NFC antenna. So this is the magic of what makes..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a person’s hands disassembling a white payment terminal, revealing its internal circuit board and screen, as Mike MacLennan, General Manager of Hardware at Square, explains in a clear, explanatory male voice how thin the NFC antenna is. A female narrator then clarifies that the card and reader represent "passive and active connections," with the reader being active. Mike, wearing a light gray button-up shirt and glasses in a modern office setting with exposed brick walls and large windows, demonstrates by tapping a black chip card against the terminal. The terminal displays a blue screen with the prompt '$1.00 Tap, Insert or Swipe', showing animated graphics of 'ACTIVE' with a lightning bolt, '13.56 MHz' frequency, and a '<4 cm' distance requirement. The narrator elaborates that the reader is active due to its power and ability to initiate radio communication, while the card is passive, lacking power and needing an active source to communicate, a principle also applied to phone taps. Mike further explains that the terminal's internal coil is actively searching for a card within a specific region. The narrator adds that this communication uses a radio frequency specific to NFC devices, effective only within a very short range, up to about 4 centimeters. The scene then shifts to a stylized graphic of a blue card labeled 'STATIC DATA' with placeholder information, as the narrator concludes by explaining that when the reader finds a card, it requests payment information stored in the card's chip, including "static data" which remains constant. The WSJ logo is consistently displayed in the top right corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a stylized digital graphic of a blue credit card labeled 'STATIC DATA,' featuring placeholder numbers and a chip against a vibrant, abstract background. A female voice explains this represents static data like account numbers and expiration dates sent from a card's magnetic stripe when swiped. The card flips to reveal a magnetic stripe, then returns to the front, emphasizing its static nature. This transitions into a sequence of rapidly changing numeric strings, suggesting dynamic data or encryption, as the female voice clarifies that chip cards also send a 'cryptogram,' a unique string of numbers to verify card validity. The scene then cuts to Mike MacLennan, General Manager of Hardware at Square, speaking in a modern office. He explains how the card collects information from the reader, combines it with card data using a cryptographic key to create a unique transaction number, making it hard to replicate fraudulently. He demonstrates a black chip card being tapped against a white payment terminal, which displays '$1.00 Tap, Insert or Swipe' and then animated graphics indicating 'ACTIVE' and '13.56 MHz' frequency. MacLennan then disassembles the payment terminal, revealing its internal components. He holds up the 'PAYMENT BOARD,' a small circuit board with a gold-colored chip, which the female voice identifies as sorting data from the card into an encrypted package. He then displays the 'MAIN BOARD,' a smaller, black circuit board, as the female voice states that the encrypted package is then sent off to this board. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated diagram illustrating the payment processing flow, showing a blue card tapped on a terminal, sending data to a server stack, then to major card networks (Visa, Amex, Mastercard), and finally to a bank, as Mike MacLennan, General Manager of Hardware at Square, explains in a clear, explanatory voice that this data is sent to 'our servers,' then to 'the networks,' and finally to 'the issuer' for approval. The scene transitions to MacLennan, wearing a light gray button-up shirt and glasses, in a modern office with exposed brick walls, holding a small black 'PAYMENT BOARD' and explaining that 'once you get to this board, it's all encrypted and protected information,' ensuring a 'high degree of security.' A female voice interjects, stating that 'all of that happens in just a few seconds, faster than inserting your chip.' MacLennan then demonstrates a white Payment Terminal, showing a side-by-side comparison of two transactions taking 1.50 seconds and 2.58 seconds, explaining that when you 'insert a chip card into a reader, there's a few back and forth in that communication,' whereas 'in contactless, there's just fewer back and forth,' making it 'more efficient, more streamlined.' The female voice returns, emphasizing that 'card networks and security experts say that this is also more secure than other ways you can use your card,' specifically noting that 'unlike swiping, your static data is better protected during that communication' and that contactless is 'not susceptible to malware.' The animation returns, showing a blue card being tapped, then swiped with a red 'X' to indicate the outdated method, visually reinforcing the security points. The final scene shows a hand placing a blue card on a white table at an outdoor market stall, surrounded by goods like cheese and honey, with a 'PASTA & VINO' sign, showcasing a real-world application.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a customer in a yellow sweater tapping a card on a payment terminal at a wooden counter, a small potted cactus nearby, as a female narrator explains how "this tap-to-pay technology has taken off in the last few years," partly due to the pandemic's push for contactless. A black screen with white text then highlights '3X CONTACTLESS PAYMENTS ON SQUARE DEVICES JAN. 2020–DEC. 2022', with the narrator adding that Square reported such payments tripled. The scene transitions to a red double-decker bus in London and a subway turnstile with a digital display prompting 'TAP HERE' for OMNY payments, as the narrator contrasts the UK's long-standing integration of contactless into public transportation with the US, which took "more than a decade." A vintage 2006 Mastercard ad then appears, showing a man using an early contactless terminal, accompanied by a male voice announcing, "For a whole new way to pay, Mastercard introduces PayPass." Peter Rudegair, a WSJ reporter, is then seen at a desk with a laptop displaying a WSJ article, speaking directly to the camera. He explains that the US first started issuing tap-to-pay cards in the 2000s, though it "didn't really work out" initially due to few businesses accepting them. The narrator introduces him as "a Wall Street Journal reporter who covers payments and financial technology." Peter is briefly shown typing on his laptop, which displays a document, before returning to his interview. The narrator then explains that US retailers have been slow to replace payment systems because they are "expensive to change." The video concludes with a close-up of a hand inserting a card into a payment terminal and pressing the green 'enter' key, followed by a bar chart titled 'RETAILERS ACCEPTING CONTACTLESS, 2022', showing 55% of retailers currently offer contactless payments, with the narrator confirming that "about 55% of merchants said they accepted tap-to-pay" in 2022. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand inserting a red card into a black Payment Terminal and pressing the green 'enter' key, illustrating a traditional transaction, as a calm female voiceover explains that "consumer behaviors are also difficult to change" and "the switch to contactless didn't feel urgent." The scene then shifts to a convenience store where a customer in a checkered shirt taps a blue card on a terminal for a contactless payment. WSJ Reporter Peter Rudegair, speaking in a clear journalistic tone from an office desk with a laptop displaying a WSJ article about Apple's new payment feature, describes how this technology "has often been described as a solution in search of a problem," noting that while "it might be convenient and... safe," it's "not usually enough for people to switch their behavior." The female voiceover returns, stating that "the tech still has room to grow" and its integration into "more parts of everyday life is working in its favor." This is visually supported by a close-up of a smartphone screen displaying a '$1.00' payment prompt with a 'Tap to Pay on iPhone' button, followed by a subway station scene where a hand taps a blue card on an OMNY turnstile, with the female voiceover explaining that "subway riders can now tap to pay at turnstiles." Peter Rudegair then elaborates on this "big push to get tap to pay into more transportation systems" to encourage daily use, suggesting that if people use it for their commute, they might also use it for "your coffee, your lunch, uh, your drinks," which is "the idea that the credit card companies hope happens." The video cuts back to Peter Rudegair, then to a title card with the WSJ headline 'Apple Will Let Businesses Use iPhones to Take Customer Payments', before a final close-up of the smartphone screen showing the 'Tap to Pay on iPhone' button being pressed. Throughout, the WSJ logo is visible, and the female voiceover concludes by mentioning how "Square and other companies have also recently released the functionality to use the NFC antenna in your phone as the active connection, essentially putting a reader in your pocket, which could make it easier."

She adds that it takes 'a lot of technology' to make this science possible, as visuals show a woman with dreadlocks and white earbuds outdoors, and another woman in a leopard-print top using headphones. John Rule, Consulting Engineer at Bose, in a gray suit, is then shown adjusting a mannequin head with earbuds, explaining in a clear, technical voice the '15 millionth of a second' precision needed to 'compute that inverse signal.' The narrator then states, 'Here's how they work,' as John Rule places a red Slinky toy on a table, using it to visualize 'acoustic waves.' The segment concludes with the narrator introducing John Rule's decade-long work on 'new algorithms for personalizing noise cancellation performance,' as he works at a desk with a scenic view, followed by a title card 'THE TECH BEHIND NOISE CANCELLATION' over a vibrant, abstract background with a laptop and earbuds.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with animated graphics illustrating acoustic waves, showing purple and white sine waves labeled 'ACOUSTIC WAVES' against a black background with vertical bars resembling an audio spectrum. These waves transition into vertical bar graphs, visually representing sound amplitude, as a male voice explains, "We draw them as sine waves as if something's going back and forth or up and down, but what's really happening is that these vibrations are actually propagating through the air." The scene shifts to a close-up of a hand tapping a glass, producing sound, followed by a blue sine wave labeled 'SOUND' and then a green wave labeled 'OPPOSITE' that cancels the blue wave, demonstrating sound cancellation. A female voice adds, "Because sound is just waves, you can cancel a a sound wave by introducing an equal and opposite wave." The Man in Gray Suit then draws a sine wave on a whiteboard, explaining the concept of wave interference, stating, "So what you've got is you've got an incoming sound wave and the way destructive interference works is we say, hey, if I could generate an equal and opposite signal... I could generate the signal that's the inverse of that, and if I sum those two things together, I'll be left with a flat line... and that will be interpreted by my brain as the noise having been canceled." He draws a second green wave, showing how it cancels the first, resulting in a flat line. The scene transitions to a close-up of a mannequin head with earbuds and a microphone. A different male voice then explains, "Bose was the first company to develop noise reduction technology for headphones. Dr. Bose was coming back on an overseas flight and got very frustrated by the fact that airplanes were very noisy," as the video cuts to the Older Man in Suit, sitting thoughtfully in front of a whiteboard filled with diagrams and equations. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with John Rule, Consulting Engineer at Bose, seated in a bright, modern office with large windows overlooking greenery, wearing a gray suit and white shirt. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing as he explains the initial focus of noise control on airplanes, with a pair of vintage-style headphones displayed on a stand beside him. The scene transitions to archival footage from the Smithsonian Institution, showing a pilot and a young child in an aircraft cockpit, both wearing bulky aviation headphones, as a female voiceover notes that pilots wore early prototypes in 1986, emphasizing that while the technology has evolved, the basic science of noise cancellation remains constant. The video then cuts to a close-up of John Rule from behind as he puts on a pair of modern Bose headphones, followed by a sleek animated sequence on a black background illustrating the inner workings of active noise cancellation. The female voiceover details the process, highlighting the 'OUTER MICROPHONE' capturing ambient noise, the 'INNER MICROPHONE' detecting sound inside the earcup and checking for unexpected environmental noise, the 'DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR' running algorithms to generate an inverse wave, and the 'SPEAKER' emitting the cancelling sound while also playing desired audio. The sequence concludes with John Rule back in the office, continuing his explanation, emphasizing the incredible speed required for this process, noting that sound travels 343 meters per second and that within an earbud, the cancellation happens in about 15 microseconds (millionths of a second). He then holds up a small pair of black earbuds with blue accents, demonstrating the technology’s evolution into compact form. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with John Rule, Consulting Engineer at Bose, speaking in a modern office with large windows, wearing a gray suit and white shirt, explaining noise cancellation technology in a clear, explanatory tone, detailing how to "cancel it out before it gets in." The scene transitions to a stylized animation of human ears in purple, followed by a diagram illustrating sound waves interacting with a purple earbud, symbolizing active noise cancellation, as a female narrator explains that "everyone's ears are differently shaped" and active noise cancellation "requires algorithms that can be tuned based on the user." The narrative continues with the female narrator explaining how "Bose, for instance, does this by playing a welcome chime" (a subtle, pleasant chime is heard) and "monitoring feedback from the internal microphone to customize noise canceling filters accordingly," while a rotating pair of white Bose headphones is showcased against a clean white background, emphasizing their design. The setting shifts to a lab where a mannequin head labeled 'HEAD AND NECK by G.R.A.S.' is seated among multiple speakers, wired for audio testing, suggesting a controlled environment for sound engineering, as the narrator emphasizes that "This requires a lot of testing to make sure the algorithms work on a wide range of users in all types of noisy environments." The video then cuts to a man in a car, wearing Bose headphones, driving through a mountainous landscape, highlighting real-world use, followed by a woman with glasses, wearing a gray blazer, adjusting her headphones while working at a desk with a computer, indicating professional use, as the narrator explains that "eliminating background noise is just the first step," and the next is "figuring out what noise to remove based on the context." The focus then moves to Poppy Crum, Neuroscientist and Technologist, speaking in an office with a cityscape view, wearing a black blazer and a silver necklace. She speaks with an enthusiastic, articulate voice, stating, "I think it's really important to recognize that noise is not bad. Noise is critical to my brain, critical to my body. My brain uses it to create richer experiences, to fill in what's missing, which is all the time," with a subtle, high-frequency electronic hum audible in the background. A close-up of a precision machine part, possibly a speaker component, is shown, followed by Poppy Crum presenting in a tech-lit room, holding a tablet and pointing to a screen. The video then shows Poppy Crum standing with four other women at a Dolby event, all professionally dressed, with the Dolby logo and Synchrony Bank branding visible in the background, as the female narrator informs us that "Poppy Crum was previously the chief scientist at Dolby Laboratories, where she worked for more than a decade," leading teams focused on "emergent technologies and platforms that impact sensory systems." Finally, Poppy Crum is seen on stage, gesturing while speaking in front of a large screen displaying a white soundwave, with the audience visible in the foreground, emphasizing her role as a speaker at a technology conference, as she begins to speak, "When you think about noise..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a workshop where the Man in Dark Shirt, wearing glasses and a dark button-up shirt, inspects a large, carbon-fiber camera body with a visible blue-tinted sensor on a wooden workbench labeled 'DOVETAIL + ORF', next to a large lens. A female narrator explains that "those pixels are wasted on a normal rectangular sensor, since a circle doesn't take up the full frame." The Man in Dark Shirt, speaking with a clear, explanatory voice, elaborates that "that circle captured from that lens is projected onto the sensor in a circular format and that circle fits perfectly in a square sensor rather than a rectangular sensor." The female narrator adds that "the lens and sensor come together to create the massive and super clear field of view needed for the sphere screen." The scene transitions to an outdoor setting, showing a wide view from inside a large dome structure, revealing a lush green landscape under a bright sky, as the narrator states, "To test this, we took the camera just outside the camera lab." The Man in Dark Shirt is then seen outdoors, operating the complex spherical camera rig, which the narrator identifies as "Big Sky," mounted on a wheeled cart. A 'SMALLHD' monitor displays a distorted, spherical fisheye view of the surroundings with overlaid red and green calibration lines. The narrator compares this to "how a camera with a flat lens and smaller sensor saw this area," highlighting the expansive view of "Big Sky." The camera rig, with multiple lenses and a large sensor module, is shown in full as the man adjusts it. The monitor continues to display the fisheye view, now with a white arrow pointing to a person in the distance, indicating tracking or focus. The narrator emphasizes that "the field of view reaches almost behind the lens," and mentions, "Here, you can see a glimpse of Deenan standing just off to the side behind Big Sky," likely referring to the Man in White Shirt. The scene shifts to a wider outdoor shot of the park, with trees, grass, and concrete pathways, before returning to the monitor, which now shows a more stable, centered fisheye view. The Man in Dark Shirt, holding the camera rig, explains the operational technique: "Instead of shooting horizontally, we tilt up so that we can capture the full field of view that is natural in sphere for where you're sitting." The Man in White Shirt operates a control cart nearby, and the narrator concludes that "the tilt helps the camera line up with the horizon." The final shot shows the monitor with the fisheye view, now with a blue vertical line and red/green calibration lines, suggesting precise alignment for 360-degree capture. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a mesmerizing underwater scene inside the Giant Spherical Structure, where manta rays glide through a deep blue, immersive environment projected onto the dome’s interior, with rows of empty seats visible below, as a female narrator explains how thousands of vertical and horizontal lights translate camera-captured pixels into an image. The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying '16,000 PIXELS' over a vibrant purple and pink spherical background, emphasizing the high-resolution display technology, with the narrator contrasting this with an iPhone screen's 2500 by 1000 pixels. Next, Deanan Dasilva, Principal at Big Sky Technology, Sphere Entertainment, appears in a workshop setting, gesturing as he speaks beside a large professional camera rig, with a monitor displaying a circular calibration pattern behind him, explaining that "you need a lot of pixels, a lot of detail everywhere" because "an audience member might be looking in this direction, another one might be looking in this direction, so everywhere on the screen is an important part of the screen." The video then cuts to an aerial view of the MSG Sphere in Las Vegas at dusk, its massive dome illuminated with a dynamic, colorful projection of a human eye and a smiling emoji, with the text 'hello world' displayed, as the narrator states, "And that's not the only thing that makes Sphere so immersive." She continues, describing how "holes in the panel allow for sound to pass through, since the speakers are behind the screen, and different sounds can emit from different parts of the screen at the same time," accompanied by a deep rumbling sound effect. This is followed by a vertical TikTok clip showing a crowd inside the venue, showered in falling confetti, with the source credited as 'TIKTOK/@SPHEREVEGAS', as the rumbling sound fades into ambient crowd noise. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping, cinematic view of a massive, snow-covered mountain range under a bright blue sky, with a large, dark stadium seating area in the foreground, accompanied by dramatic, cinematic instrumental music. A female narrator explains that Aranofsky and his team had to think carefully about their shots. The scene transitions to a close-up of a man with a beard and glasses, wearing a light gray hoodie, speaking in a video call interface. An unidentified male voice (Male Voice 1) explains the difficulty of shooting with 'Big Sky,' where 'everything is sharp' and mistakes are evident. The narrative then shifts to an aerial perspective from a helicopter flying over the same mountain range, with a spherical camera rig mounted on the front, capturing 360-degree footage. The female narrator describes a shot of a climber on the Alps that required trial and error. The man in the gray hoodie (Male Voice 2) emphasizes the importance of capturing the 'depth of the mountains' to convey the environment's risk. The female narrator identifies the man in the gray hoodie as Andrew Sholkind, the director of photography for Sphere. Andrew Sholkind (Male Voice 2) further describes the special feeling of being on a 'spire' in front of the landscape. The female narrator states that this footage eventually made its way to the screen in the sphere. Male Voice 1 recalls being in the edit, seeing the 4K footage, and the process of 'up-resing it to be able to see it in Big Dome.' The scene cuts to a group of people in a workshop, one wearing a VR headset, watching a monitor displaying a fisheye view of the snowy landscape. The video returns to the helicopter footage, showing the camera rig from different angles, then to a man in a dark shirt and glasses, inspecting a large professional camera with a massive lens on a tripod in a well-lit workshop. The female narrator concludes that Sphere is still perfecting some elements of the camera. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic space-themed animation showing Earth and the Moon from a spacecraft’s perspective, as a female narrator discusses making things "easier to use" and the "next big step" of getting something onto the International Space Station. This transitions into a comparison graphic emphasizing the massive scale of the Giant Spherical Structure (Las Vegas) at 366 FT next to the International Space Station at 356 FT. A male voice, likely Deanan Dasilva, then explains that "the screen is so big that the ISS can actually fit inside the screen," allowing for a realistic "spacewalk" experience, as the visual shows him, identified as Principal at Big Sky Technology, Sphere Entertainment, adjusting a large spherical camera rig labeled 'MS6 SPHERE' in a workshop. Another male voice adds that "one of the things that we're very interested in as Sphere is to use the venue and the technology for science," continuing to describe "the type of things that we're sort of curious about and will potentially explore in the future." The scene shifts to Ziad Sultan, Vice President of Personalization at Spotify, speaking in an office with colorful cubed wall panels, as upbeat electronic background music begins. The video transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying Spotify’s interface, where a user selects a song, triggering a 'Listening for music' screen. The female narrator returns, revealing that "more than 23,000 songs are Shazamed every minute in the world," posing the question of "how does it identify the music around you after recording just seconds of audio from your phone?" Abstract data visualizations—first a spectrogram with orange dots, then a grid of blue dots with connecting lines—illustrate this process as the narrator explains that "Shazam has to create a unique audio fingerprint of what you're hearing, and then search its database for a matching fingerprint," which means "simplifying sound to a bunch of data that can be processed incredibly fast." The title card 'THE TECH BEHIND SHAZAM' appears over a vibrant digital background, with the narrator concluding, "This is the tech behind Shazam." The segment concludes with a vintage Nokia 3310 phone centered on a blue concentric wave background, symbolizing the evolution of music recognition technology, as the female narrator begins to explain that "the idea behind Shazam is simple: Take the audio captured on your..." before the audio cuts off, with the upbeat electronic music still playing.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a retro-style screen displaying a grid of yellow dots, which transitions to a spectrogram overlaid with the text 'AUDIO FINGERPRINTING'. A female narrator explains the process of converting phone audio into a unique 'audio fingerprint' that computers can identify, even amidst background noise. The scene then shifts to a retail store where a person browses through CDs, as the narrator continues, detailing how Shazam's initial step involved digitizing a vast amount of music due to the absence of an existing database. Chris Barton, co-founder of Shazam, appears in an office setting with a cityscape visible behind him, speaking directly to the camera. He articulately describes how they hired '30 18-year-olds' to work 24 hours a day in shifts, manually putting CDs into computers running custom software. He explains that they built a database of '100,000 CDs,' equating to about '1.7 million songs,' by typing in every song, album title, and artist name because CDs lacked metadata. A photo of four men, including Barton, is shown, representing the early Shazam founding team. The video returns to Barton speaking, then cuts back to the CD store, emphasizing the transition from physical to digital music. A spinning CD against a dark background with binary code further symbolizes digital data. A detailed spectrogram with frequency and time axes is displayed, followed by a man in a dark shirt pointing at a large projected spectrogram in a dimly lit room, illustrating audio analysis. The female narrator returns, explaining that after digitization, songs were converted into spectrograms, which map frequency content over time and display signal power levels (loudness) represented by color. The minute concludes with a different male voice beginning to describe a 'spectrogram of a bird.' The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

Speaking with a clear American accent, he illustrates how a spectrogram of music changes as 'noise gets brought in,' such as people walking into a bar, drinking, and making sounds, resulting in the original spectrogram plus new activity. He emphasizes that the brain is very good at understanding 'superpositions of sounds,' and if humans can do it, computers can too. The video returns to the scrolling spectrogram, which continues to shift in intensity, before transitioning back to the grid of dots, now with blue and orange patterns, indicating a dynamic data representation. The grid then zooms out to reveal a larger, more complex pattern, with the text 'SONG 8' appearing at the bottom. The narrator concludes by explaining that Shazam's core technology involves matching these scatter plots, which essentially chart the most powerful signals at different frequencies over time, using important processing steps to quickly search its database for a match of these points in the proper sequence.

audio_updated_entities.Julius Smith: Emeritus Professor at Stanford University, wearing a dark cap and a green button-up shirt, actively gesturing toward a large projection screen displaying a spectrogram and sheet music in a dimly lit studio environment. He speaks with a clear American accent, explaining how a spectrogram of music changes with the introduction of noise, such as people walking into a bar and making sounds, and highlights the brain's ability to understand 'superpositions of sounds,' suggesting computers can replicate this.

It is explained by a narrator as being simplified by Shazam to focus on 'peaks' that the brain processes, transforming into a scatter plot of peaks. Julius Smith further illustrates how it visually represents the addition of noise to music, showing new activity alongside the original signal.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic grid visualization labeled 'SONG 8' from the WSJ, featuring scattered yellow, red, and blue dots on a dark blue grid, as a female narrator explains how a process handles multiple points at once without worrying about the overall sequence until the end. She then sets a scene, saying, 'Say you were in a coffee shop playing classical music,' as the video transitions to a coffee shop interior with a barista in a red apron. A close-up of a hand holding a smartphone appears, followed by a spectrogram with orange dots overlaid, which the narrator identifies as the first step: 'First, it makes a spectrogram like this.' The grid visualization reappears, showing yellow, then blue, and finally pink dots connected by lines, illustrating the narrator's description of isolating peaks and making a scatter plot, then connecting nearby peaks to form pairs. A smartphone screen displays the Shazam app searching for 'Minuet Recomposed' by Edward McCormack, while the narrator details how Shazam searches for matching pairs in a database of millions of songs to 'name the tune,' specifically mentioning 'Bach's Minuet in G major.' A group photo of the Shazam team, credited to Chris Barton, with a 'SHAZAM!' logo behind them, is shown as the narrator explains Shazam launched in 2002, 'well before the smartphone era.' Archival footage then shows people using early mobile phones, including a man in a pink shirt with a purple flip phone and a woman dancing with a phone, displaying a speech bubble 'I love that song!' and a Shazam tag for T.Rex's 'Jeepster.' A male speaker, likely Chris Barton, describes the limited functionality of these early mobile phones, stating, 'Everyone was carrying these mobile phones around everywhere and really you could only just do two things with them. You could make phone calls and you could send text messages. And I guess you could also play the game Snake.' The narrator then introduces an early pitch deck for Shazam, which was initially a service in the UK accessed by dialing. The segment concludes with a classic Nokia 3310 phone centered on a blue concentric wave background, displaying 'Menu' and the time '16:57,' symbolizing early mobile technology.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a classic Nokia 3310 phone, its green screen displaying a Shazam notification for T.Rex’s 'Jeepster' against a blue concentric wave background, as a female narrator explains that early Shazam allowed users to record 15 seconds of audio, disconnect, and receive a text with the song's name. The scene transitions to Chris Barton, co-founder of Shazam, speaking in a modern office with a cityscape visible behind him. The narrator continues, noting that while the core algorithm remained, engineers improved recognition. Barton, in his own voice, then explains the technology was "good enough that it felt like it worked most of the time and it really delighted people that it would work in these like bars and clubs and so on." He continues, detailing the need for "additional tweaks to deal with the fact that music in the real world also gets adjusted in terms of speed, tempo and pitch," citing how "DJs might adjust the speed of music when they're playing vinyl records." As he speaks, the narrative shifts to a vibrant concert scene with a band performing under colorful lights, audience members recording with phones, followed by close-ups of a DJ’s hands manipulating knobs and turntables under purple and blue lighting, visually illustrating the live music environment and challenges. A large illuminated Shazam logo is seen on an office wall, and a smartphone displays the modern Shazam app interface, as the narrator reveals Shazam wasn't an immediate hit, selling patents to fund development and not becoming profitable until 2016. A still photo of Chris Barton, credited 'COURTESY CHRIS BARTON', shows him with headphones, as the narrator mentions his four-year tenure as CEO before stepping down. The segment concludes with Barton, gesturing expressively, reflecting on Shazam's early struggles, stating it was "running through a long period of barely surviving," and was "a story of nearly failing, nearly going bankrupt, incredible resilience."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Chris Barton, Co-founder of Shazam, speaking directly to the camera in a modern office with a cityscape visible behind him. Wearing a light blue button-up shirt, he explains in a clear, articulate male voice that Shazam 'didn't really hit a hockey stick of incredible user adoption until the App Store launched in 2008.' A female narrator then states, 'Eventually, Apple bought Shazam in 2018,' as the scene transitions to a smartphone displaying the Shazam app interface with an overlaid Apple logo, followed by a Wall Street Journal title card announcing 'Apple Acquires Shazam and Its Song-Recognition App' dated December 11, 2017. The narrator continues, highlighting Shazam as 'one of the most popular free music apps in the App Store.' Chris Barton's voice returns, discussing the evolution of music consumption: 'Once you're paying for unlimited streaming of music, what is it you're actually going to listen to? Because you have access to everything as much as you want.' He elaborates that 'Shazam solves one form of discovery,' allowing users to discover music 'in your daily life.' The female narrator then explains that 'The technology behind Shazam has grown significantly since Apple's acquisition in 2018,' giving it access to 'Apple Music's large library,' and that 'Today, Shazam can identify most of the Apple Music catalog of over 100 million songs.' Chris Barton is then seen demonstrating the Shazam app on his phone, holding it up as it displays 'Listening for music,' then recognized songs like 'Después de la Playa' by Bad Bunny and 'Wild Child' by The Black Keys with Apple Music buttons, and a history screen showing 'Riptide' and 'Free.' He remarks, 'We created an app many years before apps actually existed. Part of the art of a startup is the timing aspect, and so fortunately we found a way to survive through those tough years.' The video then shows the Shazam icon integrated into Apple’s Control Center, with Chris Barton smiling and nodding. The segment concludes with black credit screens listing various contributors and sources, accompanied by light, upbeat instrumental background music.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with Chris Barton, Co-founder of Shazam, speaking directly to the camera in a modern office setting with a cityscape visible through large windows behind him. The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying the Shazam app interface with the word 'Listening' and the Shazam logo, overlaid with a large white Apple logo, indicating Apple’s acquisition of Shazam. A title card from The Wall Street Journal appears, reading 'Apple Acquires Shazam and Its Song-Recognition App,' dated December 11, 2017, with the byline 'Tripp Mickle.' The video returns to Chris Barton, who continues speaking and gesturing. He then demonstrates the Shazam app on his phone, holding it up to listen for music. The screen shows the app’s interface, including a 'Listening for music' prompt, followed by a display of recognized songs like 'Después de la Playa' by Bad Bunny and 'Wild Child' by The Black Keys, with Apple Music buttons. The app’s history screen is shown, listing songs such as 'Riptide' by Vance Joy and 'Free' by The Machine. The video then displays the Shazam icon within Apple’s Control Center, emphasizing integration. Chris Barton smiles and nods as he concludes his explanation. The video ends with black screens listing credits for footage and animation, including names like Karl Mollohan, Corey Eisenstein, Sean Harvey, Ryan Trefes, Michael Tabb, Luca Depardon, and others, with sources such as Google, Getty Images, and Shazam.
